I finally figured out what was going on.

I run RabbitMQ's mochiweb interface on localhost and then use stunnel to provide access via HTTPS to remote users where I also require they present a client certificate.

On remote hosts running flower I use stunnel to connect to mochiweb over HTTPS and then provide an HTTP service on localhost for flower to use.  I have flower bind to localhost and once again use stunnel to provide access via HTTPS to remote users with a client certificate.

The version of stunnel I'm running is a few releases behind and it was misbehaving when I would restart flower.  Sometimes stunnel would exit, but other times it would just not respond properly.  This resulted in behavior like workers initially showing up as offline when they were really online, or workers reporting in very slowly.  I found that if I restarted stunnel when I was experiencing this behavior, then the workers showed up immediately with the proper status.

I know this setup is complicated, but I like to use multiple layers of access controls for these types of services.

I'm not a fan of:

1. Needless state, `loaded = False`
2. Guessing function names, `function_name = 'load_' + binding.lower() + '()'`
3. `exec`

(1) makes it difficult to read; having to continue reading before understanding what `loaded` does. (2) Means that the name of a function is stored twice; both in the function, and once in the list of orders. Complicates maintenance. Finally (3) is just no.

The current approach (#9) isn't pretty, but it is easy to understand and maintain. We should hold on to those properties.

Right now, the behavior is implemented such that the inner-most block determines what happens. However, I don't like it.  I think it makes any use of `allow_cancellation` a ticking time-bomb in the code-especially if it's buried in a library function.   See my earlier comment for an example of such code.

Big picture:  I don't think a singe inner-most`allow_cancellation` should enable cancellation when nested `defer_cancellation` operations have been applied.    If some outer `defer_cancellation` is in effect, it was done for a reason--it shouldn't be something that can be arbitrarily overridden by accident within the innards of a library function that happens to use `allow_cancellation` someplace.

This is being caused by a bug in the dark magic of a dark corner of experimental curio metaprogramming.   Given the experimental nature of the project and to satisfy my curiosity about where it might lead (against my better judgement), I've pushed a patch that should fix this.  Let me know.

I wanted to add that as long as async/await are NOT permitted in the context-manager code block, this feature seems no-more dangerous to me than calling a synchronous function in a thread.   One of the main features of Curio is that it tries pretty hard to separate async code into its own environment.  Virtually every interesting feature of Curio requires the explicit use of async/await.  If those are disallowed, then there's no meaningful way for the code block in the context manager to interact with Curio from a separate thread.

Just thinking about this further, it seems kind of weird to me that a `Task` would be used as a `Future`.  Normally, the return value of a `Task` (i.e., returned by `join()`) is the final return value of a coroutine.   A `set_result()` method seems like a strange out-of-band way to set a result that's different from this.  If anything, it exists in conflict with it.   I think I'd probably need to see a concrete example of what's being attempted with it.

Independently of that, there are still probably scenarios where having a custom Task class could be useful.  Although, I wonder if merely down-classing an instance would be sufficient:

    class MyTask(Task):
           ...

     task = await spawn(coro)
     task.__class__ = MyTask

> How does a recv() call work with threads? If data is available, it is returned immediately. If not, the calling thread is blocked by the OS kernel and a new thread is scheduled.

> How does a recv() call with with Curio? If data is available, it is returned immediately. If not, the calling task is blocked by the Curio kernel and a new task is scheduled.

This comparison doesn't really hold up.

First, that's not how network I/O scheduling works on real kernels.  Once your thread has context switched into the kernel, it might get scheduled out if too many other threads are ready to run, whether a result is available from your particular syscall or not.  This can happen as a result of a call to `recv` or `send` or `select`, but it might also happen as a result of a timer interrupt.  The kernel scheduler is invoked _very_ regularly.

By contrast, in a cooperatively-scheduled framework like Curio's, user-space scheduling is all that we have, so being more careful about scheduling decisions and taking the opportunity of a `recv` or `send` call seems reasonable, since it may be all you get.

I should point out that interactive performance problems will happen _anyway_ - see for example this Stack Overflow question about interactive performance on Twisted's SSH server becoming untenable when one user is hogging all the bandwidth - http://stackoverflow.com/questions/40601119/python-ssh-server-twisted-conch-takes-up-high-cpu-usage-when-a-large-number-of/40627446 - the ability to do scheduling _at all_ is only the first step to doing some kind of fair scheduling.

Yeah, there was a lot of noodling and soul-searching when that pattern was figured out (and I still have reservations about parts of it.)

In the end, the decision was that the gains (adding new, simple functionality to will takes <2 min, including deploy) outweighed the losses (tighter coupling, harder to share plugins, etc.)

Hubot takes a similar tack, though it's pure maintain-your-fork.

What I'd hoped to achieve with will was a sort of middle ground.  You'd have your own codebase that used will, get all the core will improvements just by pip upgrading, and you had (hopefully) the flex to use external, abstracted modules if you wanted to.  Your template suggestion actually makes that last bit a reality.

The rationale (if you're interested) behind keeping it simpler (no list of installable plugins, etc) was my frustration when trying to set up a bot with the current solutions.  They all hit me as over-architected monstrosities whose code had eclipsed their documentation.  There were install-and-configure plugins via chat capabilities, dynamic everything, and super strong abstractions.

But in the day to day of setting them up and using them, my team hated them.  Adding the stuff we wanted was a pain, even though the actual code we wanted to add was a simple, few-line script.

Thus was born will.  A bit more tightly coupled, with some convention over configuration, but super easy to add the functionality you wanted, fast.

Anyhow, somewhere, there should be a manifesto of sorts on all this stuff, but if you're interested, there it is!

Finally, thanks again for suggesting this, and making will even more flexible for varying philosophies!

My bad, the correct code is:

```python
ticket_metric = zenpy.tickets.metrics(ticket_id=2)
```

This returns the ticket metric for the ticket with an id of 2. The `ticket_metric` endpoint is a primary endpoint, so in the code:

```python
zenpy.ticket_metrics(ticket_id=1)
```
the `ticket_id` keyword argument is ignored, leading to all ticket metrics being returned (which would be a lot). The `ticket_metric` endpoint is to be used when you want to retrieve a `TicketMetric` by it's id, the `tickets.metric` endpoint is for when you want to retrieve a `TicketMetric` by it's associated ticket's id, which is what you want to do.

The Qsys files could have a basic version detection performed on them - the newer Pro files contains the line 'tool="QsysPro"' as part of the XML structure, whilst the Std edition ones do not. Perhaps when the backend encounters a QSYS file, it could attempt to parse the basic XML data to see if the QsysPro line is specified? Assuming the backend also identifies the available quartus version then throwing some sort of exception on a mismatch should be possible (the current Quartus backend raises a RuntimeError for missing required options, so doing something similar for mismatched files should work).

Working through the implications of this, I think it might start touching on the thoughts @imphil had in issue #103 . Imagine a core that implemented an Arria 10 memory controller, including the interactions with the EMIF. The EMIF would probably come from a QSYS file (using the Arria 10 External Memory Interfaces library component), whilst a more user friendly interface might come from custom RTL. A user depending on this core's interface for their project may be using either Quartus Std or Quartus Pro, which would therefore require both a Std and a Pro QSYS implementation being available. I think CAPI2 has a use flags system to allow for conditional inclusion of filesets, so if a use flag existed for "QuartusPro" and "QuartusStd" then that would make this process very easy? You could further imagine a scenario where this interface proves useful to people on Xilinx devices, and that conditionally selecting the Xilinx equivalent of QSYS files might be necessary, whilst maintaining the RTL interface. Is this something that useflags currently supports?

You could do some magic with dup/dup2, but I think if you want to translate between tcp and udp the only way to do it is to manually receive and send each packet - the translation has to happen in user code.

In order to make this work you'll want to use threads. I don't remember the python threading API exactly off the top of my head, but something like this:

```python
import threading

class TranslateThread(threading.Thread):
  def __init__(self, nc_src, nc_dest):
    self.nc_src = nc_src
    self.nc_dst = nc_dst

  def run(self):
    while True: # or possibly a termination condition
      self.nc_dst.send(self.nc_src.recv())

tcp_server = TCPServer(src)
for tcp_client in tcp_server:
  print('Accepted client %s:%d' % tcp_client.peer)
  udp_client = Netcat(dest, udp=True)
  TranslateThread(udp_client, tcp_client).start()
  TranslateThread(tcp_client, udp_client).start()
```

Hi, you don’t have to set use_ssl=True because the initial connection is not an ssl connection. You must also issue a c.start_tls() operation before binding because your server doesn’t allow for clear text connections.

Anyway it's not True that ssl over port 636 is deprecated as is stated in the DigitalOcean guide. The advantage of using a dedicated ssl port is that the whole connection is encrypted. With start_tls() only what is sent after this operation is encrypted, so it must be issued at the very beginning of the connection.

Also, if you don’t use a real certificate you can omit the Tls() object and use the default Tls() configuration that accept any certificate.

Let me know if this helps you.

This sounds like generally a bad idea, depending on what you are trying to accomplish. Most likely you'll want to use something like https://github.com/wbond/certvalidator. That said, the rest of this comment explains the API and how you could do what you are asking for.

The API isn't specifically documented anywhere but in the code. You can expect the following attributes to work on all supported platforms:

```python
class Certificate():
    """
    Container for the (crypto library) representation of a certificate
    """

    # An asn1crypto.x509.Certificate() object
    asn1 = None

    @property
    def algorithm(self):
        """
        :return:
            A unicode string of "rsa", "dsa" or "ec"
        """

    @property
    def curve(self):
        """
        :return:
            A unicode string of EC curve name
        """

    @property
    def bit_size(self):
        """
        :return:
            The number of bits in the public key, as an integer
        """

    @property
    def byte_size(self):
        """
        :return:
            The number of bytes in the public key, as an integer
        """

    @property
    def public_key(self):
        """
        :return:
            The PublicKey object for the public key this certificate contains
        """

    @property
    def self_signed(self):
        """
        :return:
            A boolean - if the certificate is self-signed
        """

```

To compare the issuer and subject, you'd need to access the `.asn1` attribute and compare the ASN.1 values of the `asn1crypto.x509.Certificate` object. It has properties for `.issuer` and `.subject` (https://github.com/wbond/asn1crypto/blob/master/asn1crypto/x509.py#L2522-L2538), and the asn1crypto implementation takes into account the RFC rules for comparing names.

Just be aware that even though the issuer and subject are the same does not mean the cert is self-signed. You really have to verify the cryptographic signature. The `.self_signed` attribute of the `oscrypto.asymmetric.Certificate` object will do this, but otherwise doesn't do any of the RFC 5280 checks to ensure the certificate conforms to the RFC.

`schedule.every(10).minutes.do(self.task_one)`

Hi all,

the .gitattributes is a great way to handle line endings. It is very easy to introduce, as one just need to create the .gitattributes file with '* text'. Afterwards all files are normalized with LF during commit and checkout as is. 
Drawback: One rely on the git magic layer for text/binary detection.
If you don't want that, you have to specify every file type explicitely.

Apart from that, I realized, that it is the same for other tools and it seems, that there is no easy way to choose the line ending yourself in python. As far as I understood it would be neccessary to open the file in binary mode and replace the line ending character.

Therefore it seems the best solution to use .gitattributes for now.

@ralphschreyer this is a great question. Unfortunately there's not a *simple* solution to this problem since the requests get made asynchronously. However, I just pushed an update to the stocks_example app which implements a caching mechanism that you might find useful: https://github.com/adamhajari/spyre/blob/master/examples/stocks_example.py

Here's how it works:
1. initialize two caching variables, one for your data and one for your params
2. have your getData method update the cached data and the cached params (after dropping the output_id key in the params dict).
3. have your getPlot method check it's params against the cached params (again, make sure to drop the output_id key). While the cached params and it's params differ, have it wait.
4. once the getPlot method's params match the cached params, have it use the cached data.

So yes, it turns hidden directories not appearing in `listdir('/proc')` but which are still `os.stat()`-able refer to running Linux threads:
http://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/72693/a-hidden-process-what-it-is
http://www.linuxquestions.org/questions/linux-general-1/hidden-directories-under-proc-what-are-they-for-proc-23142-a-433785/

So the question is how to pre-emptively distinguish a process from a thread. 
We need this at least for fixing `psutil.pid_exists` which should return `False` instead. It seems the easiest option is to check for `pid in os.listdir('/proc')`. I'm not completely happy with this because `pid_exists()` is supposed to provide a faster implementation than `pid in psutil.pids()`. 

Another possibility is to read `/proc/pid/status`: if `Pid` and `Tgid` values are different then we're dealing with a thread (and hence we should return False), but maybe this is even slower than the first solution (will have to make a benchmark).

Sorry, been busy. I briefly took a look at this and it seems more complex. Looking for a number is not enough, see:
https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/issues/338

```
major minor  #blocks  name

 202        0   19922944 xvda
 202       16    1048576 xvdb 
```

#### 1) "but the middleware currently responds to all OPTIONS requests itself":

 oops - PR is always welcome :)

#### 2) passing on or answer exclusively in case of preflight:
On one hand if a preflight request is detected it would be sufficient if this wsgi layer answers the request. On the other hand such behaviour would contradict the wsgi stackability. But then again if no other layer answers then the typically innermost wsgi layer (usually the real application) will throw an 404 (not sure). So i'd tend to let the wsgicors layer handle a preflight request completely - your thoughts?

#### 3) Allow -header:
as i understand it, the Allow header is uri specific. So it's much more fine-grained that just the dsn:port distinction in CORS. So yes, when requesting, say, 
GET example.com/some/resource 
you could return 
Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE
Allow: GET, POST, DELETE 

where Allow specifies the methods allowed for the resource example.com/some/resource
and Access-Control-Allow-Methods for everything below example.com

I _guess_ Allow should be a subset of or equal to the Access-Control-Allow-Methods.
Now whether this is honoured on the client-side is another question :)

in your example you would just do:
```
producer.send_messages(topic, *message)
```
and it will send all messages together.

But kafka is a distributed, partitioned log system. So your group of 20,000 messages will very likely get split across all available partitions -- you wont actually be sending all messages together in a single batch.

Is there a reason you want direct control over the batching system? Typically users let the kafka client handle batching in the background.

well... `layer->nexgtLayer->getGradInput()` actually returns `layer->gradOutput`, so it will have the same size as `layer->output`.

For two layers, layer1, and layer2, we have, going forwards:

- layer1->input
- layer1->output == layer2->input
- layer2->output

Then, backwards we have:

- layer2->gradOutput (same size as layer2->output)
- layer2->gradInput == layer1->gradOutput . (same size as layer2->input, and layer1->output)
- layer1->gradInput . (same size as layer1->input)

@mkleehammer so, to summarise, the SQL type (and length/precision) could be obtained by:

- Explicit setting
- SQLDescribeParam
- Python type

in order of precedence. However, I'm not sure if the benefits of adding an explicit cache here would outweigh the complexity and edge-cases that it could introduce, since ODBC already has a mechanism for doing something similar: prepared statements. SQLDescribeParam may be slow, but for a single parameterised query there is no choice to call it if you want to bind with the same SQL type, and if you want to execute that same query more than once, you should be using prepared statements/executemany() in which case it is still only a single SQLDescribeParam call per parameter.

In particular, the case where the schema changes is important to consider. In such a case, pyODBC-cached information may not longer be correct.

Hmm,  I think we can do this and #771 without needing to add code to the modules themselves.  My take is that the modules should be a simple as possible and we add any complication into py3/core etc.

So for example we might add a `py3.requires(['one', 'of', 'these', 'commands'])` if we wanted that functionality. But we'd also catch the `command_output()` error and generate the error automatically.  We'd need to change the actual exception thrown but that wouldn't break stuff.

This also allows us to globally change the behaviour if needed.  It may take a while to get the pieces in place but it also would work for modules that we don't even know about eg. custom user ones.

Anyhow it'd also save having to add 12 odd lines of code to every module.

1(a) No, the 5 is the port number for UDP traffic so you will see tuples more like `('192.168.0.12', 47808)`.  Device instance numbers are usually larger values, in the case of a nearby large institutional network, they are numbers like 3406712, where 3406 is a code for the building, 34067 is the BACnet network number, and 3406712 is a device on that network.  It's following a general plan of YYYYXNN, _but that's a local convention and every BACnet network is different_.

1(b).  If you are using the UDP layer in BACpypes rather than writing directly to sockets, the destination address should be an instance of `LocalBroadcastAddress` and the UDP layer will translate that into the appropriate IP address based on the binding (in your case using `x.x.x.255` for a `x.x.x.0/24` network).

2. There are three general layers of abstraction in the BACpypes library, the *LAN* layer like UDP and virtual LANs, the "network" layer that handles the BACnet networking layer of BACnet routers and routing traffic, and the *application* layer that sends and receives the confirmed and unconfirmed requests, and simple and complex acknowledgements.  The application layer has an additional sub-layer that deals with segmented messages, chopping up big requests into pieces and reassembling them.

I'm not sure I understand what layer you are expecting your application "sit".  Are you writing a client application (that will be sending requests to other BACnet devices for data), a server application (that will be presenting itself on the network as a BACnet device), or both?  You may be attempting to write an application that implements some portion of an entire stack, where your program is going to be responsible for all of the intermediate encoding and decoding.

The layers of abstraction are intended to shield the application layer developer from the network layer, just like the socket library is designed to shield protocol implementations from the IP network layer. Similarly, the application layer has three layers of classes, each one more specific than the others.  A `ReadPropertyRequest` is a kind of `ConfirmedRequestPDU` which is in turn a kind of `APDU`, so depending on where/when you debug the contents of the packet, you'll be seeing a more abstract or more concrete version of the packet.

Because things like "source" and "destination" depend on the layer and context, it's hard to answer your other questions.

From the standard...

> *J.5.2 BBMD Operation - Foreign Devices*
> 
> In order for a foreign device to fully participate in the activities of a B/IP network, the device must register itself with a BBMD serving one of the IP subnets comprising that network. "Full participation" implies the ability to send and receive both directed and broadcast messages.

I interpret this to mean that both unicast and broadcast messages should fail when it is not registered.  In theory this also means that the stack should probably block all incoming traffic until a registration goes out and an positive acknowledgement is returned.

I have some very old code that implemented a mostly hideous hardware handshake (RTS/CTS) half-duplex master/slave protocol using the [pySerial](https://github.com/pyserial/pyserial) library.  Because it didn't have a `select()` socket-like API all the send/receive calls were done in a separate thread.  I was using something that is probably called the "legacy API" since this was back in the late Python2.4 and early Python2.5 days!

In one world you are basically correct.  Assuming your serial protocol is master/slave and you are providing the slave implementation, then at the time it knows it has a complete PDU it creates a read/write property request, stuffs it in an IOCB and sends it to the `ApplicationIOController` instance.

However, it might be easier for you to "directly" reference the object and property values.  Again on the slave side, if you get a `read oat\n` message then maybe your code would be as simple as `return str(some_map['oat'].presentValue) + '\n'` assuming that `some_map['oat'] = AnalogValueObject(...)` is someplace in the initialization of your application.  Note that `getattr(obj, 'presentValue')` is very similar to `obj.ReadProperty('presentValue')` but different, and `setattr(obj, 'presentValue', 75.3)` is not the same as `obj.WriteProperty('presentValue', 75.3)`.

So it's not quite the same as attaching an IOController to an object or a property of an object.  I'm guessing that you want to keep the BACnet/IP stack happily running while this is going on, and that mapping the serial traffic to BACnet services is not a key feature of your design so much as making sure the serial side and BACnet/IP side have a unified view of object values, correct?

(It's kinda sad that BACpypes doesn't have asyncio support support yet.)

@evilplanet, I am glad to hear that you were able to correctly use both `-O` and `--dicdir` together. 

`natto-py` is meant to use the options in the same manner as they are passed to the `mecab` command-line, in order to be as familiar as possible, and so that is why you have to specify all of the options at instantiation time. 

Beside using a single, long options string, you can also use key-value pairs in `dict`.

For example, these are equivalent:

    # a single, long string
    MeCab('--dicdir=/usr/local/lib/mecab/dic/mecab-ko-dic -Owakati')

    # a dict with key-values
    MeCab({ 'dicdir': '/usr/local/lib/mecab/dic/mecab-ko-dic',
            'output_format_type': 'wakati' })

You can review the various `MeCab` options in the project Wiki's [Appendix B: Supported MeCab Options](https://github.com/buruzaemon/natto-py/wiki/Supported-MeCab-Options).

The error that you supplied is thrown in the generated bindings when a new instance of the object is created. PyangBind generally treats member variables in the structure as immutable, so that we create a new version of the object rather than trying to mutate the existing one - the creation of the new object throws `ValueError` in the case that some error is returned during instantiation of the object with `type(val, args)`.

So, to debug the class above, it seems like a good step would be to remove that `ValueError` being caught, and seeing what the underlying error that is returned is.

Happy to help if you can point me to the code.

Okay, rich has set up the Galaxy class. The idea is you assign each Galaxy in your lens system to an instance of the galaxy class, including how its light and mass are modeled. For a simple lens and source this would be:

lens_galaxy = Galaxy(redshift=0.5, light_profile=Sersic(...), mass_profile=SIE(...))
source_galaxy = Galaxy(redshift=1.0, light_profile=Sersic(...), mass_profile=None)

This defines a GalaxyCollection, which automatically orders the galaxies in redshift order:

galaxies = GalaxyCollection(source_galaxy, lens_galaxy)

The GalaxyCollection class should now go through our list of galaxies and do the following:

1) Go to the lowest redshift galaxy not yet analysed (this is automatically the first galaxy in GalaxyCollection).
2) if light_profile != None, fit the light profile of the Galaxy using the current lens-plane coordinates (for the first galaxy this will be the uniform grid of coordinates defined by the observed image).
3) if mass_profile != None, compute the deflection angles of this galaxy and use them to update the lens-plane coordinates. 
4) Rinse and repeat for every Galaxy.

**What if light_profile is not a parametric function, but a Pixelization?**

To recover a 'normal' analysis where the lens is fitted by a light profile, and the source by a pixel-grid, we should be able to put something like:

lens_galaxy = Galaxy(redshift=0.5, light_profile=Sersic(...), mass_profile=SIE(...))
source_galaxy = Galaxy(redshift=1.0, light_profile=AdaptivePixelization, mass_profile=None)

This will repeat the steps above, but when the code his the source galaxy light profile, it will set up an AdaptivePixelization (this is the source-pixel grid, F and H matrices). 

**What is there are multiple source galaxies?**

Lets say we have two source galaxies, which in our image are quadruply image giant arcs so both need fitting using a pixel-grid (noting also that the mass of the first deflects the second):

lens_galaxy = Galaxy(redshift=0.5, light_profile=Sersic(...), mass_profile=SIE(...))
lens_and_source_galaxy = Galaxy(redshift=0.75, light_profile=AdaptivePixelization, mass_profile=SIE(...))
source_galaxy = Galaxy(redshift=1.0, light_profile=AdaptivePixelization, mass_profile=None)

Two new things will happen in this set up:

1) After we derive the source pixelization for lens_and_source_galaxy, we will use its SIE mass model to compute a new set of deflection angles and update the lens-plane coordinates. Note that, unlike single redshift lens systems, the different redshifts of these galaxies means a ratio of angular diameter distances must be taken when computing the deflection angles.

2) We will then set up the pixelization for source_galaxy, using these new lens-plane coordinates.

So, for multiple source planes, each Pixelization class must set up the source pixelization and mappings between image pixels and source pixels (e.g. F and H). However, the inversion **must** be performed at the end of the analysis, once all source-planes have been set up. This ensures we fit the image using all source-planes simultaneously. As a consequence, we must ensure each source plane begins its source pixel indexing from the value of the previous source plane. 

Thus, there is a crucial aspect of our design outlined above. We will define Pixelization classes that represent the mappings between each image pixel and set of source-planes. However, the final reconstruction of the source must be performed **after** we have set up the source-plane for every source in our GalaxyCollection.

Hello and apologies for the late reply. I just pushed python-evdev 0.6.3 to pypi with a potential fix for this. You should now be able to use the `--evdev-headers` option to the `build_ecodes` distutils command. It can be done in one go:

```
python setup.py build \
  build_ecodes --evdev-headers $SYSROOT/input.h:$SYSROOT/input-event-codes.h \
  build_ext --include-dirs  $SYSROOT/ \
  install
```

With bitbake, you could just set the following in your recipe:

```
DISTUTILS_BUILD_ARGS = "build_ecodes --evdev-headers $STAGING_INCDIR/input.h:$STAGING_INCDIR/input-event-codes.h build_ext --include-dirs  $STAGING_INCDIR/" 
```

Please let me know if this works or if you have other ideas.

Kind regards,
Georgi

Doxygen does not export deprecated tags. Instead, it produces a separate page called `Deprecated List` in which there is a text which contains all deprecated members. You can see 'page deprecated' mentioned at the very end of the [tinyxml documentation](http://breathe.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tinyxml.html).

An example of generated xml follows:

```xml
<?xml version='1.0' encoding='UTF-8' standalone='no'?>
<doxygen xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" xsi:noNamespaceSchemaLocation="compound.xsd"
         version="1.8.14">
    <compounddef id="deprecated" kind="page">
        <compoundname>deprecated</compoundname>
        <title>Deprecated List</title>
        <briefdescription>
        </briefdescription>
        <detaileddescription>
            <para>
                <variablelist>
                    <varlistentry>
                        <term><anchor id="deprecated_1_deprecated000001"/>Class <ref
                                refid="class_n_q_b3_1_1_t_extractor_base" kindref="compound">NQB3::TExtractorBase
                        </ref>
                        </term>
                    </varlistentry>
                    <listitem>
                        <para>
                            <ref refid="class_n_q_b3_1_1_t_extractor_base" kindref="compound">TExtractorBase</ref>
                            is deprecated
                        </para>
                    </listitem>
                </variablelist>
            </para>
        </detaileddescription>
    </compounddef>
</doxygen>
```

As you can see, it is a simple text. So, the choice is to either ask doxygen authors to add this information to the generated xml or parse this page and extract relevant info. The latter approach may be easier and faster to implement, however, it may cause problems if doxygen authors decide to change layout of this page.

There is a somewhat close bug in the doxygen bugtracker: https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=624495. The bug looks like an abandoned one, however; last change in 2012.

It is in fact illegal to offer draw during the opponent's turn but engines are a somewhat special case because of the FIDE rules:
```
A player wishing to offer a draw shall do so after having made a move on the chessboard and before stopping his clock and starting the opponent’s clock. An offer at any other time during play is still valid but Article 12.6 must be considered. No conditions can be attached to the offer. In both cases the offer cannot be withdrawn and remains valid until the opponent accepts it, rejects it orally, rejects it by touching a piece with the intention of moving or capturing it, or the game is concluded in some other way.
```
Now obviously engines don't press clocks and GUIs are designed to press clocks the instant they receive a best move from the engine. It is clear engines can't be handled the same way as the FIDE rules dictate. I have sent a message to HGM asking for some advice on the matter.

That's an interesting idea, @jrennstich.  The syntax you give above is how pandoc supports definition lists, and pandoc has a DefinitionList block element in its json format.  I can imagine writing pandoc-defnos or some such thing.

Below are some thoughts on a possible implementation.

The first requirement would be to attach attributes to a definitions list. @jgm [recommends](https://github.com/jgm/pandoc/issues/1959) using bracketed spans for this purpose (see also [bracketed spans](http://pandoc.org/MANUAL.html#spans) in the pandoc README).  As such, the syntax would look something like
~~~~
[Term]{#def:id}

:   definition text
~~~~

where `Term` and `definition text` could be whatever you want; as per the other pandoc-xnos filters, `id` is a unique identifier.

References would look like `@def:id`.

I have already checked to see how pandoc processes the above markdown, and it is manageable.

However, there would need to be some differences from the other pandoc-xnos filters.  Where should the reference name come from?  In pandoc-eqnos it comes from a yaml variable (with the sensible default of 'eq.').  I'm thinking for this it should come from what is in the square brackets, above.

The reason you're having trouble with doc files [is that doc files use `antiword` to extract the text](https://github.com/deanmalmgren/textract/blob/master/textract/parsers/doc_parser.py#L9), which is a command line utility available on most unix systems. 

By default, [the pdf parser uses `pdftotext` to extract text](https://github.com/deanmalmgren/textract/blob/master/textract/parsers/pdf_parser.py#L17), which is another command line utility that is installable on most unix systems. With pdf documents there are [other methods available though](https://github.com/deanmalmgren/textract/blob/master/textract/parsers/pdf_parser.py#L29-L32). In particular, you should be able to use the `pdfminer` method to be able to process your pdfs.

Answering your questions:

(1) Right now, I am somewhat inclined to say "no", but please continue discussing your ideas. Once I better understand the relation between merging into `pip` and dependency tracking, you might be able to convince me. My current inclination to "no" is based on the following observations:

 - With #32 and #41, `pip-review` is currently moving in a direction where it defines less of its own logic and relies more on `pip list`. Adding dependency tracking to `pip-review` would be a move in the opposite direction.
 - As of version 9, `pip list` has `--process-dependency-links` and `--not-required` flags. I never tried these options and I do not know when these options first appeared, but the existence of these options does suggest that `pip` actually implements some form of dependency tracking.
 - `pip-compile` (from the current pip-tools package) does include a dependency tracker. So depending on the exact use case, `pip-review` might not need to offer dependency tracking because `pip-compile` already does it.

Note that the last point is more or less neutral. If we do decide to add dependency tracking, we could take advantage of the existing implementation in `pip-compile`.

I think permissions management and NFS are going to be a bigger concern than speed when it comes to choosing between the per-directory and single-file cache designs.

With a single-file cache design, it's fairly straightforward to say:

1. In an active virtual environment, store the cache in a defined location within the venv
2. Outside a venv, if there's a per-user data caching directory defined and writable, use that (so the cache is shared between processes run by that user).
3. Otherwise write the cache to a per-process temporary directory.

With per-directory caching, you'd have a harder time dealing with system site-packages directories that are owned by the root user, and an even higher chance of at least some of the caches ending up being stored on network drives.

There is a bit of history on this one...

In version 1.X, I always sent the headers, but when adding regex support (which I needed for subdomains in a project), I realized that it doesn't really make sense.

So in version 2, I removed the send_always option and more strictly followed the W3 spec (https://github.com/corydolphin/flask-cors/blob/2.1.3/flask_cors/core.py#L115).


I now am of the opinion that it is better for us to always return the headers we can. I know of no browser which would react negatively to a CORS header on a request which it issued no Origin for.

The main complication (which was entirely self inflicted), is that by allowing regular expressions for matching origins, it means that we cannot always do the right thing. So I added a heuristic that basically returns all non-regex Origins for requests without an Origin header.

What do you think? I'd love feedback.

1. I think the best way to include `*args` and `**kwargs` is to include them directly in the `optimize()` function rather than having a positional argument with name `args=()` or `kwargs={}`:

```python
def optimize(f, iters, print_step, verbose, *args, **kwargs):
    # Stuff...
    self.swarm.current_cost = objective_func(self.swarm.position, *args, **kwargs)
    self.swarm.pbest_cost = objective_func(self.swarm.pbest_pos, *args, **kwargs)
```

Hi @nnnet , thanks for the feedback! :+1: Unfortunately most of your requests fall outside of the scope of this project. Basically, the `gplearn` project goals is to offer a fairly streamlined interface into GP with a scikit-learn style API. Adding a large amount of complexity to what can be achieved for more specific needs means leaving that behind and moving towards a fully customizable architecture. There are other packages out there that fill that need at the cost of more set-up time by the user.

I don't have any immediate plans for adding a "parameter" type of node for the next release, though that might be something that I consider in a future release down the road. One obvious complication would be that you would need to constrain certain inputs to each function which adds a good amount of complexity to creating user-defined functions. Anyhow, I'll tag this feature request and leave it open for any other feedback from other users.

Your example of a moving average function does not really fit within the existing framework. There is no concept of "time" in the input datasets, so that would be tricky to implement without the user-defined function pointing to an external column of timestamps (outside of X). That should be workable within the existing function definition framework though (without the evolving parameters part) although subsampling throws a spanner in the works.

At this stage I do not plan on adding support for returning multiple columns from a function. That deviates too much from the tree-based structure of the base programs and would make the recursion of tree more expensive potentially.

You may be able to implement such a model-based function within the master branch function framework. Again, no immediate plans for parameter nodes. But I may revisit this after the next release if there is sufficient demand.

Thanks again for your feedback, I hope you understand the goals of the project do not align well with many of these specific requests.

In that example one will see in the first code block:
```
libraries_path = os.path.join(os.environ['PySpice_examples_path'], 'libraries')
spice_library = SpiceLibrary(libraries_path)
```
What this code is asking for is  the location of  https://github.com/FabriceSalvaire/PySpice/tree/master/examples/libraries in PySpice's github directory. 

So in order to get the examples working one needs to copy said folder to a local location. I personally reformat the code as follows

```
libraries_path ='SpiceLib/'
spice_library = SpiceLibrary(libraries_path)
```
where `'SpiceLib/'` is where I keep my spice model files for the cirucit I am working on. Right now the Spice Library parser `SpiceLibrary` supports '.lib', '.mod', '.lib@xyce', '.mod@xyce' files contianing spice model as per the docs https://pyspice.fabrice-salvaire.fr/api/PySpice/Spice/Library.html?highlight=spicelibrary

You're right, this probably does cause problems in Sanic. I have just never come across those problems.
Some background: In Flask-Cors, they use the `MultiDict` class from the Werkzeug library.
The `MultiDict` class in Workzeug is case _insensitive_, however the simpler lightweight `MultiDict` class in Sanic is case _sensitive_. When I ported Flask-Cors to Sanic-Cors, the plugin was written in a way that it only worked if the headers are in a case _insensitive_ dict, hence why I used `CIDict` here instead.
I have since removed a lot of the case-variability in the Sanic-Cors plugin, so we may be able to move away from `CIDict` now. I will do some testing over the next few days and we'll see.

I'm thinking that ideally I'd like an implementation that's provider-agnostic; some abstract base class that defines an initializer and a send_metric method that takes a string service name, string limit name, usage value and limit value, and sends the metric. From the CLI, I assume it would be something like ``--metric-provider=ClassName`` and ``--metric-option=name=value`` (using [StoreKeyValuePair](https://github.com/jantman/awslimitchecker/blob/0.7.0/awslimitchecker/utils.py#L47) for the options). The idea being that adding a new metric provider (other than Prometheus or statsd/graphite) would be as simple as adding a class that meets a specified interface.

I can probably get started on an initial implementation of this in the next week or two... My main points would be:

* Clearly documented how to create a class that implements a metric provider, and add tests for it.
* MetricProvider abstract base class
* The CLI ``-h|--help`` option should list all known metric providers
* Metric providers classes should have a documentation string describing CLI options, which would be printed via a help-like option (i.e. ``--metric-provider=ClassName --metric-help``) or else just list and print all of them via something like ``--metric-providers`` or ``--metric-help``.
* The actual hook implementation should happen in get_usage, get_limits and check_thresholds; there should be an option (CLI and on the AwsLimitChecker( ) class) to rescue exceptions from the metric provider and just log them.
* ``runner`` parses all ``--metric-option`` into a dict (key/value); passes to AwsLimitChecker as ``metric_provider`` and ``metric_options`` kwargs.

So a few things, using the example from the WSJ that you messaged me with earlier:

You should be able to your info from the current package though.  It just recodes the metadata into a dict -- there are no special rules or processing. Try using the commandline like this:

	>>> from metadata_parser import MetadataParser
	>>> from pprint import pprint
	>>> url = 'http://www.wsj.com/articles/police-widen-search-for-tennessee-officers-killer-person-of-interest-held-1438539422'
	>>> parsed = MetadataParser(url=url)
	>>> pprint(parsed.metadata)

That will show the dict below.  it's just catching the metadata into a dict that is managed by several known schemas (og, dc, meta, raw page data, etc)

The parsed object caches a lot of data

	>>> dir(parsed)
	>>> dir(parsed.response)
	>>> print parsed.response.text

If you're using this as part of a script, you can use the python debugger (standard library) to set breakpoints

	import pdb
	...
	parsed = MetadataParser(url=url)
	pdb.set_trace()


That will pause the script and let you inspect the objects/state.

these two are useful:

1. `parsed.soup`  # the BeautifulSoup object
2. `parsed.response`  # the actual response object (raw html, headers, etc)

I would check the parsed object to see what the response returns.

Several websites, including the WSJ, have aggressive Firewalls that are used for ratelimiting and spider identification.  I recall the WSJ have a very low threshold and instead of raising an error, it gives a 200OK and served a CAPTCHA page.  If that is still the case, you may need to examine the response object to see if it's a real page or not.

If you do need to write advanced rules, the `parsed.soup` is the actual `BeautifulSoup` document.

Now, if you look at the dict (copied below), you will hopefully notice an small difference -- the WSJ uses both an `article:` and `article.` prefix.  You're searching for `article:published` but the data is in `article.published`.  

This is most likely because the `article:` namespace is typically used by a hybrid of OpenGraph and proprietary Facebook tags, while `article.` doesn't respond to anything that is well known.

Doing a bit of digging...  The following things seem on ONLY appear on pages from the wsj:

* this header tag `<!-- TESLA DESKTOP16 V1 -->`
* a dotted namespace with keys like `article.origheadline` and `page.content.source`

I would guess that the big problem you're facing is that the published time for WSJ articles appears in a proprietary tag.  You can access it easily with this package, but it's going to be in the WSJ's `article.published` field, not the OpenGraph(http://ogp.me/#type_article) `article:published_time` that you're expecting.

I would start with adding a simple `specs` cached property to the filterset (basically move https://github.com/miki725/django-url-filter/blob/master/url_filter/filtersets/base.py#L314-L324 to a cached property) and then use that property in the `filter()` call. Also since there could be many possible ways to render those specs, I think it would make sense to create some sort of renderer which would take as input the filter specs and output some string output which could be HTML or could be something else. For example one renderer could output as HTML description list.

API might looks something like this:

```python
>>> class ShopFilterSet(FilterSet):
...     name = Filter(form_field=forms.CharField(label='Name', max_length=32))
...     employees = Filter(form_field=forms.IntegerField(label='Employees', min_value=0))

>>> fs = ShopFilterSet(QueryDict('name=Starbucks&employees__gt=5')

>>> f = DescriptionListFormatter(fs.specs).render()
<dl>
     <dt>Char</dt>
     <dd>value</dt>
     <dt>Int</dt>
     <dd>5</dd>
</dl>

>>> f = DescriptionListFormatter(fs.specs, with_lookups=True).render()
<dl>
     <dt>Char</dt>
     <dd>value</dt>
     <dt>Int</dt>
     <dd>> 5</dd>
</dl>
```

Hello,

There are several problems with this code.

> handle=context.open()

This does not open an USB device, but does what the context manager already did: it initialises libusb1 internal stuctures.

You should instead call `openByVendorIDAndProductID` to get an opened handle for a specific USB device.
Please see the documentation (`pydoc usb1` or equivalent) for more details (and more variants).

> OSError: [WinError 126] The specified module could not be found

Python cannot find libusb. It must be installed where the dynamic library loader can find it. Please check libusb 1.0.x documentation (the underlying C library) on how to install it. Note that windows requires extra steps to be able to use devices, installing libusb 1.0.x itself is not enough.

As there is no python-libusb1 bug here, I close this report. Feel free to reopen if you have any issue specific to python-libusb1.

My interest is that "absolute" performance is hard to put in perspective. If now python-libusb1 takes a negligible amount of time compared to time actually spent in libusb1, then there is little to gain by optimising further.

On a higher level, if you are not doing a one-shot transfer but something recurring, you may want to try the async API: setup a handful of transfer object, define a function to call on completion which will process received data (ideally not spending too long, maybe piping it to another process) or will push the next data chunk when sending. This way, you can keep the USB bus busy even when the CPU is busy resubmitting the transfer. This is what I do in my [USB protocol analyser driver](https://github.com/vpelletier/ITI1480A-linux/blob/master/iti1480a/capture.py) and I can receive 43MB/s with the bus and/or the device being the bottleneck (USB 2.0 device).

I'm storing different translation suggestion for one language by simple use different language_codes for each. Creating a unique language_code is also quit simple by adding an incremental counter. But the problem is to use it in parallel functions, because you have to lock this small logic part for each creation which causes a big overhead. My proposal would be to enlarge the max_length so that you can store a unique code without any logic that have to be looked. The max size would be 36 characters if we want to support python core uuid (https://pymotw.com/3/uuid/index.html 36 would be the length of the hex value) or 22 if we want to support a library like https://github.com/skorokithakis/shortuuid

It will work indeed. It does have the drawback of generating one additional query per object displayed in the admin. If at some point this becomes problematic, remember there is always the possibility of overriding the `apply_select_related` to prefetch all translations at once.

And should you have any issues doing that, simply re-open this :)

It isn't possible to dynamically set choices in your `models.py` files, because Django doesn't let you run queries before all models have been imported.

However, you should be able to set this after import by setting the field's `choices` attribute. See these for details:

* https://stackoverflow.com/questions/3419997/creating-a-dynamic-choice-field
* http://blog.yawd.eu/2011/allow-lazy-dynamic-choices-djangos-model-fields

Actually, it seems there is no problem with finding your email template by mail_templated app. The error is probably going from Mandrill. I think the problem is caused by the `template_name` property set on the message object. This is from Djrill documentation:
> If template_name is set, Djrill will use Mandrill’s messages/send-template API, and will ignore any body text set on the EmailMessage.

As a workaround you can unset the `template_name` property before sending the message. Could you please try this instead of `send_mail`:
```
from mail_templated import EmailMessage
message = EmailMessage(
    'email/hello.tpl', {'user': user}, from_email, [user.email], render=True)
del message.template_name
message.send()
```
If this works, I'll implement more compact method for such cases.

Thanks in advance :)

The full function is below.  
`
    def get_batch_result_iter(self, job_id, batch_id, parse_csv=False, query_job=False,
                              logger=None):
        """
        Return a line interator over the contents of a batch result document. If
        csv=True then parses the first line as the csv header and the iterator
        returns dicts.
        """
        status = self.batch_status(job_id, batch_id)
        if status['state'] != 'Completed':
            return None
        elif logger:
            if 'numberRecordsProcessed' in status:
                logger("Bulk batch %d processed %s records" %
                       (batch_id, status['numberRecordsProcessed']))
            if 'numberRecordsFailed' in status:
                failed = int(status['numberRecordsFailed'])
                if failed > 0:
                    logger("Bulk batch %d had %d failed records" %
                           (batch_id, failed))

        uri = self.endpoint + \
            "/job/%s/batch/%s/result" % (job_id, batch_id)
        r = requests.get(uri, headers=self.headers(), stream=True)
        
        if query_job == True:
            #https://github.com/heroku/salesforce-bulk/issues/20
            result_id = r.text.split("<result>")[1].split("</result>")[0]
            uri = self.endpoint + \
                "/job/%s/batch/%s/result/%s" % (job_id, batch_id, result_id)
            r = requests.get(uri, headers=self.headers(), stream=True)

        if parse_csv:
            return csv.DictReader(r.iter_lines(chunk_size=2048), delimiter=",",
                                  quotechar='"')
        else:
            return r.iter_lines(chunk_size=2048)
`

Regardless, there needs to be a timeout for when the application has stopped processing the queue, so that seems like a good step 1.

1. self.ramp(self.conv(x)) vs mx.gluon.nn.Conv1D(activation='relu')(x) Yes. The latter applies a relu activation to the output of Conv1D.

2. mx.gluon.nn.Sequential is for grouping multiple layers into a block. Usually you don't need to explicitly define each layer as a class attribute. You can create a list to store all the layers you want to group and use a for loop to add all list elements into mx.gluon.nn.Sequential object.

3. Yes. Call forward on mx.gluon.nn.Sequential is equal to call forward on all child blocks, with topological order of computation graph.

After some research, the truth is that we should not have used the + symbol in the URL because '+' is expected to replace spaces (https://www.w3.org/TR/html401/interact/forms.html#h-17.13.4.1). 
If you have any idea where I could add a test, I will be happy to help.
Thanks!

>  I can't find a good doc to explain the proper bind/connect

Because of zmq's abstractions, every connection order and direction is *valid*. So it *shouldn't* be an issue for SUB to bind and PUB to connect. However, I have seen some issues mainly because this scenario is uncommon, and thus bugs are less likely to be found/prioritized. That said, it is rare that I would recommend using PUB-SUB if the receiver is the socket that binds.

Some tips about which socket should connect and which should bind:

1. if there is only one instance of one side, it should bind.
2. If a side has many instances, or they come and go often, it should connect.

Some tips for when to choose PUB-SUB:

1. Messages should be dropped if there are no SUB sockets connected and subscribed.
2. You have many (or sometimes zero) sockets receiving messages.

If the following conditions are met, PUB-SUB is probably *not* right for you:

1. messages should not be dropped
2. you have only one receiver
3. that receiver is ~always running

Often when I see PUB connecting and SUB binding, what makes more sense is the sink pattern, the bottom half of [ventillator-sink in the Guide](http://zguide.zeromq.org/py:all#Divide-and-Conquer). That is, many PUSH sockets connecting to one PULL socket.

A PUSH-PULL sink has these differences from using PUB-SUB in the same arrangement:

1. messages will not be dropped
2. the receiver should always be running
3. if the receiver is not ready or not yet connected, messages will wait/block instead of being dropped.

The ProcessDevice is a stand-alone fully working program "black box" that assumes SIGTERM and SIGINT have a certain behavior, as there is no other way to kill it. The user should not be able to change the signal handling for ProcessDevice so it's able to finalize it properly. If the user overrides the signals then there is absolutely no way to bring it down save for SIGKILL.

A side-bug of this is leaving open orphaned processes if your main script dies when signals are over-ridden which results in bind() errors when you restart your program. That is why detaching from current process group is also not good.

It seems like there might be a problem with `tf.nn.ctc_beam_search_decoder()` and its treatment of repeated characters.

The sequence `A` `L` `L` `*` `L` (where '`*`' is the blank label) will become 
- `A` `L` if `merge_repeated=True`.
- `A` `L`  `L`  `L` if `merge_repeated=False`.

So, unless the `L`'s happen to align with the frames, it is hard to impossible to get words with characters repeated. A solution for this needs to be found. 

Currently I assume the language model fixes this, but this has to be verified when the language model is in place. Until then this seems like a bug.

for a fixed search_k: more trees is always better, up until the point where you run out of memory on your computer

for a fixed number of trees: a higher search_k means higher accuracy but at the cost of slower search time

basically pick as large of a n_trees as you can afford, and then pick search_k to be a good tradeoff between accuracy and speed

After some additional review, the rdbms CopyToTable abstract task we inherit from provides a method for performing operations (i.e. queries) prior to the table copy `init_copy`. Perhaps we should add `post_copy` to the ancestor.

`print boto3.__version__`

I see your point, but in this particular example, the order of arguments for `gsutil cp` isn't that flexible. In particular, your example would fail as the `gsutil` command would be:
```
gsutil -m cp <bucket_url> <destination_filename> -L <manifest.log>
```

Note the correct order is:
```
gsutil -m cp -L <manifest.log> <bucket_url> <destination_filename>
```
In theory, one could format `-L <manifest.log>` as a unit to solve this problem, but I think with just 2 cases, it's probably overkill as it would make the command harder to process for our minds, especially considering that FISS is a public-facing tool.

I think I'm narrowing the issue to the fact that input is supposed to be bytes (former `str()`), but all string litterals are unicode over there.

But because the `handle_process_output()` function is called from `_get_fetch_info_from_stderr()` with `decode_stream=False`, the pump function is not decoding the `bytes` to `str`, and later in the `progress_handler` it fails.

I switched all `decode_streams` to `True` in `git.remote` and it's not crashing anymore.

In either cases, with that solution it's yelling:

```
cmd.py                     585 DEBUG    Popen(['git', 'pull', '--progress', '-v', 'bitbucket', 'master'], cwd=/tmp/tmp4z86lqw9, universal_newlines=True, shell=None)
remote.py                  660 DEBUG    Fetch head lines do not match lines provided via progress information
length of progress lines 2 should be equal to lines in FETCH_HEAD file 1
Will ignore extra progress lines or fetch head lines.
```

but I believe it's unrelated to the issue at hand.

So we've got two solutions, etiher make the progress handler bytes and str agnostic (as I first suggested), or change the remote.fetch calls to `handle_process_output()`.

Hi, sorry you ran into this issue

For context, you are having this problem because you ran pipenv with `sudo` privileges. You should never do this, this is not supported or recommended and it should never be necessary.  Doing something as `sudo` will _always_ use root permission and therefore mess with whatever files are created. If you want to fix this problem you will have to remove these thigns using `sudo` again

Pipenv does not create `pip-selfcheck` or any other pip related files, but it does make pipenv related files and other files in your virtualenv -- this is how virtualenvs work.  When you run them with root permissions they get created as root.  Therefore you need to be root to remove them.

For more support please see stackoverflow

cc @brandur @stripe/api-libraries 

Hey @koobs, thanks for reporting this. I've investigated a bit and here's what I found.

As you noticed, we currently force `mock == 1.0.1`. This version does **not** define `assert_not_called()`. It seems calling a non-existent method on a `Mock` object will return another `Mock` object, so the two tests that use `assert_not_called()` do not actually assert anything! 

The `assert_not_called()` method is defined in mock 1.1.0 and above. When upgrading mock to a version that supports `assert_not_called()`, the tests fail *properly*: the error is in the tested methods and not in the tests themselves.

For instance, in `test_non_recursive_save()`, calling `charge.save()` should not trigger a request to the API, but it does. After digging a bit, I think the problem is in `StripeObject.serialize()`. This method will always return a dict with a key for every sub-object and an empty dict as the value, even if the sub-object was unchanged. As a result, `UpdateableAPIResource.save()` will send an update request.

This seems non-trivial to fix, as we also need to be able to differentiate between a not-updated sub-object and a sub-object that we explicitly want to set to an empty dict to reset it (see `UpdateableAPIResourceTests.test_array_none()`.

To illustrate:

    >>> import stripe
    >>> stripe.api_key = 'sk_test_...'
    >>> ch = stripe.Charge.create(amount=400, currency='usd', customer='cus_...')
    >>> ch.serialize(None)
    {u'fraud_details': {}, u'source': {u'metadata': {}}, u'refunds': {}, u'metadata': {}}```
    >>> # The above should be {}
    >>> ch.save()
    >>> # The above does send a POST update request to the API with an empty body

Hey @null-none, it seems like you're trying to fix a Python 2 vs. 3 compatibility issue. If you try to import the raw `stripe/api_requestor.py` file from Python 3, it will fail because `urlparse` no longer exists in Python 3's standard library, and `urllib.parse` should be used instead.

That said, you should not be importing the library's raw source code directly with Python 3. We use `2to3` to automatically update the code for Python 3 (cf. [here](https://github.com/stripe/stripe-python/blob/023d742a163d4a814ac63ac4533137b2917db0eb/setup.py#L61)).

You should either install the library using `pip` or, if you want to install from source, run `python setup.py install` to make sure the source files are processed by `2to3`.

I'm going to close the issue but feel free to reply if you have any questions!

I copied the Zhang-Suen reference verbatim from the old function (the Python wrapper had it already) … but a word of warning:
I did not personally verify that the LUT implements Zhang-Suen thinning. I just reimplemented the Python version as Cython …
As I stated earlier, Zhang-Suen thinning is normally implemented by directly evaluating the connectedness of a neighborhood. With this implementation, each pixel neighborhood is converted into a unique index for the LUT, whose value is then used to judge whether a pixel should be removed or not. … I did not verify that the manual way and the LUT yield identical results … but that's how it was implemented before in skimage …

@sciunto I'm refering to metrics typically used for evaluation of segmentation quality (http://host.robots.ox.ac.uk/pascal/VOC/voc2012/htmldoc/devkit_doc.html#SECTION00064000000000000000, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jaccard_index), object detection quality (http://host.robots.ox.ac.uk/pascal/VOC/voc2012/htmldoc/devkit_doc.html#SECTION00054000000000000000, the ones from the article above), and multi-class classification quality (accuracy, precision, recall, http://host.robots.ox.ac.uk/pascal/VOC/voc2012/htmldoc/devkit_doc.html#SECTION00044000000000000000).

Here is an example of metric from the aforementioned article:
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1315589/24858855/ab45ea22-1df6-11e7-872f-ecb433c9df90.png)
Given 2 sets of bounding boxes (reference (in solid line) and estimated (in dashed line)), we perform bipartite graph matching with the highest overlapping area (in gray). Then, using this matching we compute the Jaccard index. This method gives a number which allow to compare object detection algorithms, and judge on which one is better with respect to considered method.

@lnielsen This is also [what we are currently doing in B2Share](https://github.com/EUDAT-B2SHARE/b2share/blob/33e49cdc6036e608acde18b78df848ce41f802dd/b2share/modules/deposit/api.py#L107). But the problem is not that it is not possible, it is that we have IMHO a confusing and inefficient behavior by lazily creating the bucket when `record.files` is called.

*Example of performance issues:*
in `invenio-deposit` [we call `deposit.files`](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-deposit/blob/352336ba2b7786369b445fb3c4b34669e2ffad3a/invenio_deposit/api.py#L249) to check if the deposit has files. This will create a bucket which will be discarded right away if there are no files (see [here](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-records-files/blob/c46676d91ed66444981fb44d16849d51204d347f/invenio_records_files/api.py#L243) and [here](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-deposit/blob/352336ba2b7786369b445fb3c4b34669e2ffad3a/invenio_deposit/api.py#L502)). Thus we have multiple calls to the DB without any need as [we already knew here](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-records-files/blob/c46676d91ed66444981fb44d16849d51204d347f/invenio_records_files/api.py#L239) that the deposit has no files.

*Example of confusing and error prone behavior:*
Record serializers are called [outside of the main transaction](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-records-rest/blob/master/invenio_records_rest/views.py#L507) as they are not supposed to change the data. Thus they can't do something like the following example because the bucket id would be discarded as soon as the response is sent:
```python
if record.files:
  links['file_upload_link'] = url_for(
    'invenio_files_rest.bucket_api',
    bucket_id=record.files.bucket
  )
```
And even if the bucket id was not used, calling `record.files` to check files existence would still call the DB at each serialization.

We could say that `record.files` should not be used to just check if there are any files, but as seen above we already do it in our code. It's confusing if sometime it is ok to use it that way and other times it is not a good idea.

Note that it is possible to check the bucket existence by using `invenio-records-files` models directly. One simple solution which I would IMHO find better is to always use the `Bucket` and `RecordsBuckets` models to create the buckets, and use `invenio-records-files` only for *reading* the buckets or checking that they exist, but not for creating them.

I haven't  found a fix for the Kivy Launcher but I did find a way to turn off automatic update for this app only.
Go to the Googlr Play Store app on your phone and open it (start her up). Click on the menu button icon at the top left corner of the screen.
Scrpll down to 'settings'.
Make sure either of the two automatic update options is selected.  Obviously one of them has to be already selected.  Otherwise we wouldn't be having this conversation. 
Then back out to the main screen and open the menu choices again. (either by swping the screen from left to right or by clicking on the menu item at the top left.
Choose 'my apps and games'.   Then scroll through your list of installed apps until you find that demon possessed program called Kivy Launcher.  Click on it and when the overview screen appears, hit the tiny icon at the top right corner.  There will be only one option showing called 'auto update' deselect it and you're good to go.

> Why does Travis kick off two builds every time we push to the branch? One for the PR, and one for the most recent push... Isn't that redundant? How can we change that?

There are two options in the Travis settings for the repo: "Build branch updates" and "Build pull request updates". You can disable building branch updates to remove the duplicates. 

For zipline, we wanted to remove the duplicates but also test master when there's been a merge or commits made, so we used a different method: we specify in the [travis config](https://github.com/quantopian/zipline/blob/0a481d4d6caf1bb185e68826cb8c4ac155c03069/.travis.yml#L80-L82) to filter down the branches built to only master.

+1 for this, but maybe you should separate registering global logger handlers and task logger handlers
```python
    register_logger_signal(client, loglevel=logging.ERROR)
    register_task_logger_signal(client, loglevel=logging.ERROR)
```
 This can be done by passing celery signal as argument and creating some proxy-functions

```python
...
from celery.signals import after_setup_logger, after_setup_task_logger
...

def _register_logger_signal(signal, client, logger=None, loglevel=logging.ERROR):
    filter_ = CeleryFilter()

    handler = SentryHandler(client)
    handler.setLevel(loglevel)
    handler.addFilter(filter_)

    def process_logger_event(sender, logger, loglevel, logfile, format,
                             colorize, **kw):
        # Attempt to find an existing SentryHandler, and if it exists ensure
        # that the CeleryFilter is installed.
        # If one is found, we do not attempt to install another one.
        for h in logger.handlers:
            if type(h) == SentryHandler:
                h.addFilter(filter_)
                return False

        logger.addHandler(handler)

    signal.connect(process_logger_event, weak=False)

def register_logger_signal(client, logger=None, loglevel=logging.ERROR):
    _register_logger_signal(after_setup_logger, client, logger, loglevel)

def register_task_logger_signal(client, logger=None, loglevel=logging.ERROR):
    _register_logger_signal(after_setup_task_logger, client, logger, loglevel)

```

OK, this is quite an impressive piece of work.  Sadly I feel that I'm not the most qualified version to review the updates to the config backend.  Not sure what to do about that.

It might be worth mentioning the GREAT3-like config scripts in the README where we talk about demos?

Certainly most use cases would only do one or the other, so I don't think we need to make it super easy to do both with the same Atmosphere object.  

Maybe delay making the screens until you know whether or not to include the high-k part, and then issue a warning if they then go on to also do the other method.

Interesting.  I think there are a couple of things going on here.  The SED is basically reporting that it isn't defined where you need it to be defined in order to compute a flux through a Bandpass.

Part of this may be that the Bandpass in question extends down to 400 nm, even though the bulk of the throughput doesn't actually start until ~700 nm.  So using the .truncate method [here](https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/blob/master/galsim/scene.py#L577) might be appropriate.  Actually, I'd probably just pre-thin and truncate this file (documenting what you do in its header) in the share directory so this doesn't need to happen when running scene.py.

More critically though, the SEDs aren't being read in right.  They're missing a `wave_type='ang'` kwarg [here](https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/blob/master/galsim/scene.py#L586) and below.  I probably should have caught this in an earlier review, sorry!  What you've effectively discovered is that GalSim refuses to compute the F814 flux for a z=26 elliptical spectrum, which I don't think is that surprising.

Finally, we should probably update the bandpass and sed files in the share dir so that they use the newer version of .thin().  I remembered to do this for examples/data/, but not for share.

I've sped up the test_sensor.py unittests to ~ 1 second if __name__ == __main__.  It's about 70 seconds otherwise, mostly in the routine to calculate B-F slopes.  There are still two outstanding items that I'm aware of:

(1) I just put in a rough approximation of the silicon absorption length vs wavelength in Silicon.cpp.  Eventually this needs a more accurate look-up table.

(2) The whole photon shooting could be sped up dramatically if we only calculated the pixel shapes every N photons, with N~1000.  But this would require the PhotonArray class to store all of the distorted pixel shapes - on the order of 40-100 doubles for each pixel.  Mike - is this feasible?  If so, I could work on this.

>I never looked into it, but I wonder if interactive python can be made to print the str version of things rather than the repr for unassigned return values...

Just wanted to mention that I did find a way to do this for the regular python interpretter (doesn't seem to quite work in IPython though):
```
import sys
def p(x):
    print str(x)
sys.displayhook = p
```
You can place this in your $PYTHONSTARTUP file.

For IPython, I think something like on [this blogpost](https://www.safaribooksonline.com/blog/2014/02/11/altering-display-existing-classes-ipython/) might work, but I haven't figured out a good way to make this load automatically yet.

I ran rgc.yaml, and there are two issues I want to bring up.

1. It still doesn't seem to be triggering the "too much noise to whiten" exception that I thought we were expecting.  Do you know how often that should happen?  Maybe it's just because the first file (which is the one I've been using for testing) happens to have a largish PSF, so that smooths the noise enough that it is never too large.
2. The reject function is dominating the running time.  In particular, the `getVariance` call to figure out how much noise to subtract from the var to calculate the right snr.  That call accounts for more than 1.5 hours out of a 2 hour running time (for one output file).  So I think we need to either figure out a way to speed up that function or find some other heuristic-y way to account for this noise correction that runs faster.

Small introduction how the `operationId` is handled in https://github.com/sarnowski/swagger1st :

The configured executor has a generic way of plugging in any `operationId` evaluation you need. [The default implementation](https://github.com/sarnowski/swagger1st/blob/master/src/io/sarnowski/swagger1st/core.clj#L77) resolves the `operationId` string to a full qualified Clojure function.

However, this can be configured as you like. The `resolver` takes a function that takes the argument `operationId` (actually the whole request definition) and returns a function that should be called upon request. This generic functional approach allows you to plug in every logic you might want. Some examples:

```clojure
(s1st/executor
  :resolver (fn [request-definition]
               (let [operationId (get request-definition "operationId")]
                 (function-by-name operationId))))
```

You can also think of completely different implementations like a SQL executor:

```clojure
(s1st/executor
  :resolver (fn [request-definition]
               (let [sql-query (get request-definition "operationId")]
                 (fn [request]
                   (jdbc/execture sql-query)))))
```

For this issue, there are only 2 clean ways in my opinion:

## Way 1: Abstract Idenfitier

The Swagger Spec also specifies the `operationId` as an identifier for the operation. There is no specification that this has to translate to a language specific construct. One way to tackle the language-independent integration is to make this identifier compeltely abstract and let the implementations resolve it.

    operationId: "foo"

Using a mapping table in the implementation, one is able to determine the correct action. Luckily, Clojure's map is also a function, so a map can directly be used as a resolver in swagger1st:

```clojure
(s1st/executor
  :resolver {"foo" api/my-foo-fn
             "bar" api/my-bar-fn})
```

I consider this the cleanest solution as it makes no assumption at all about the implementation.

## Way 2: Declarative Configuration

If you really want to encapsulate implementation specific information per implementation, then I would go for embedded JSON as a declarative way:

    operationId: '{"connexion": "my-py-fn", "swagger1st": "my-clj-fn"}'

This way, it is trivial for any implementation to get to is implementation specific value by just parsing JSON (which is the easiest format in any language):

```clojure
(s1st/executor
  :resolver (fn [request-definition]
               (let [operationId (get request-definition "operationId")
                     s1st-operationId (-> operationId json/parse :swagger1st)
                 (function-by-name s1st-operationId))))
```

I would discourage any idea of trying to use a turing complete templating engine to "generate" a correct string!

Another option to speed things up would be to cheat...

Currently you'll get the same results for the following code whether you use protobuf or pyrobuf:

```python
>>> t = test_message_pb2.Test()   # for pyrobuf use t = test_message_proto.Test()
>>> s = t.substruct
>>> t.Clear()   # use t.reset() in pyrobuf 0.5.4
>>> t.substruct is s
False
```

This is because an entirely new substruct object is created when the message is cleared/reset. You could cheat by simply clearing the original substruct object rather than creating a new one. Since clearing an existing object is faster than creating a new one, this will speed up message clearing.

The down side is that code like the following will have different results in pyrobuf vs protobuf:

```python
>>> t = test_message_proto.Test()
>>> s = t.substruct
>>> s.field1 = 54321
>>> t.Clear()
>>> s.field1 == 54321
True   # This will be False if you only clear the substruct rather than creating a new one
```

To implement this cheat, you could add an optional keyword argument to the `Clear`, `ClearField`, `CopyFrom` and `ParseFrom*` methods like this:

```python
def ParseFromString(self, data, size=None, reset=True, pyrobuf_do_recycle=False):
    ...
    if reset:
        if pyrobuf_do_recycle:
            self.recycle()
        else:
            self.reset()
    ...
```
