Utilisation = time busy / (time busy + time idle)

server schedules:

If for 10 mins 2 servers on duty:
 + one is busy 3 mins out of 10
 + the other is busy 1 min out of 10
Then for 5 mins 1 server is on duty:
 + that server is busy 4 mins out of 5

Then overall utilisation =
= (total busy time) / (total busy time + total idle time)
= (3 + 1 + 4) / (10 + 10 + 5)
= 8 / 25

This is equivalent to weighted averages weighted by how long they have been on duty for:

overall utilisation =
= (10/25) * (3/10) + (10/25) * (1/10) + (5/25) * (4/5)
= (3/25) + (1/25) + (4/25)
= 8 / 25

From the looks of that API, it doesn't let you do encrypted configuration variables (maybe I missed it).

Assuming that's the case, I don't think it can work. In one place or another, you have to have something be encrypted with the Travis public key, so that no one can see it except for Travis itself. Right now, we do a double encryption: we encrypt the ssh key, to `doctr_deploy_key.enc`. The key used to encrypt this file is then itself encrypted with the Travis public key, and used as an environment variable. This lets the environment variable not be enormous (the other option would be to encrypt the public key as the environment variable, which I think could work in theory, assuming there aren't size restrictions on environment variables).

However, maybe this is still doable. We don't want users to commit their fork's encrypted ssh keys to the main repo, but they could perhaps commit them to a special branch of their fork. doctr would then checkout that branch when looking for the key. That way, it doesn't have to be merged into the main repo.

By the way, Travis does have a feature that would make this whole thing a lot easier, which is the ability to upload ssh keys directly. Unfortunately, it is only available for private repositories. I've made a feature request for them to add it for all repos here https://github.com/travis-ci/travis-ci/issues/7420.

Okay everyone, how about this?

```
Close All Widgets
----------------
[ ] Save Live Widgets when Saving Notebook
[ ] Save All Widgets when Saving Notebook
----------------
Download Widget State
Export Widgets to HTML
```
The two Save options make sure that at most one is selected between them. The setting is saved with the notebook. Saving *all* widgets saves all widget state with the notebook (including possibly widgets from previous kernels or state that was loaded from the notebook). 

Toggling this option would *not* also save the notebook immediately, but would just set up things so that when the notebook *is* saved, the state is saved.
You can just create an HTML widget with an a tag with a data URI, like: ```python from ipywidgets import HTML HTML("""asdf""") ```

> Yet another way

Hello all,

@davidedelerma and I have been working on it. We both use Ubuntu 16.04.03 LTS. We created a new environment following @petewa commands from a previous comment. Using this environment, we ingested a single time slice of Landsat 8 data in a S3 bucket. There was no error in the ingestion. We loaded this data with `use_threads=False` and we did not have any problem: the data is correctly loaded, even though it is slow. Surprisingly, the instance we used had 4 vcpu, and the 4 of them were being used in the ingestion process. On the other hand, we also tried loading the data with `use_threads=True`. In this case, the jupyter notebook never finishes (at least for a reasonable amount of waiting time) loading the data, and the consumption of the vcpus is nearly 0.

In the same environment, we ingested two time slices of Landsat 8 data. We loaded these data using `use_threads=False` and `use_threads=True`, and in both cases we were able to load it. Afterwards, we ingested more data and we had in total 9 time slices of Landsat 8 data. We loaded a region of interest of these 9 time slices, due to limitations with memory, but we did not have any problem as well.

We tested all of the above with both develop and csiro/execution-engine branches, where the two yield datacube version 1.5.1.

At this point, we ask ourselves
1. What are the advantages of storing the ingested data as objects in S3 with respect to using S3 buckets as a file system for ingested data by means of software that allows to mount S3 buckets, e.g. [s3fs-fuse](https://github.com/s3fs-fuse/s3fs-fuse)?
2. How does `use_threads` actually paralellise the data access from S3? Since when we used `use_threads=False` we saw that our all vcpus were being used.

Elaborating on the point 2, we did a small benchmark of the elapsed time when loading the ingested data for a region of interest for the 9 time slices, when ingested in the S3 using the s3 driver and when ingested locally, and also testing the develop and the csiro/execution-engine branches. The results show the average time over 3 runs.

|                      | Branch develop                                            | Branch csiro/execution-engine                             |
|----------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------|
| In S3 with s3 driver | `use_threads=False`: 33.24 s, `use_threads=True`: 25.29 s | `use_threads=False`: 83.08 s, `use_threads=True`: 72.64 s |
| In EC2               | `use_threads=False`: 6.71 s, `use_threads=True`: 6.45 s   | `use_threads=False`: 6.63 s, `use_threads=True`: 6.22 s   |

We expected some problem if we loaded the ingested data in EC2 using `use_threads=True`, given a previous comment. However, it works and it is faster! On the other hand, we are also surprised to see that the csiro/execution-engine branch is faster when loading the ingested data in EC2 but much slower when the data is in S3.

There is something else that may be worth sharing. The environment we were using before was the one created by following the installation guide for Open Data Cube in the [www.opendatacube.org](https://www.opendatacube.org/data-cube-installation) website: [Data Cube Installation Guide](https://github.com/ceos-seo/data_cube_ui/blob/master/docs/datacube_install.md). However, when using the environment created with the environment file `datacube-core/.travis/environment.yaml`, there is an error with the postgres database that before we did not have:
```
sqlalchemy.exc.OperationalError: (psycopg2.OperationalError) could not connect to server: No such file or directory
	Is the server running locally and accepting
	connections on Unix domain socket "/tmp/.s.PGSQL.5432"?
```
Before we did not have a `db_hostname` in the `.datacube.conf` file. To solve this error, we had to set either `db_hostname: localhost` or `db_hostname: /var/run/postgresql` in the `.datacube.conf` file.

Could this be related to #313?

I see that the DFK is getting cleaned up while a task is still running. If you have a very simple script e.g.

```
@python_app
def foobar():
    return True

foobar()
```

where you never call result, the DFK might clean up without waiting for the task. Instead, you'd need

```
@python_app
def foobar():
    return True

foobar().result()
```

to force waiting for the result (we will fix this in the above-linked issue).

This has been fixed in #483 where we now wait until the network port becomes available before running the health check probe requests. You should not be seeing this error anymore during startup. I'm closing this issue, but please feel free to re-open or leave a comment here if the problem persists. Thanks

Since PR 436 has been merged, let me continue the discussion here. 

Is the goal to allow LoopIndexTensor as a generic operator (a special case of Gather), or to constraint its use where the index is allowed to be only the iteration variable?

In the latter case, I think that the best strategy is to use a scan/fold/reduce/map like operator: in this case, the operator will specify which axis of which tensor we are iterating over, and the "loop body graph" will take as a parameter the corresponding "LoopIndexTensor(j)" itself as a parameter rather than taking the loop-index "j" as a parameter, and there is no need to use "LoopIndexTensor". 

If there is a need to index more than one tensor (in a synchronized fashion), then we can generalize the above to a "zip" like operator (that takes multiple tensors, with an axis for each, and iterates over them in a synchronized fashion).

If we do use LoopIndexTensor, then I think its documentation should be changed and clarified (which I find confusing and ambiguous currently). It should not be restricted to "just the loop index variable".

Thank you for suggesting this feature but I have some concerns on this will negatively affect the output of the pipeline.

In particular my concerns are related to the internal logic of `velocyto`. 
`velocyto` uses evidence of exon-intron spanning reads of all the valid cells to validate introns (e.g. reads mapping to introns that never had any read mapping to the exon-intron in the whole dataset are just discarded to avoid different kinds of possible artifacts, e.g. unannoted transcripts). I am concerned that extending this check to the raw cells barcodes,  will defeat the purpose of this intron validation step and increase the background of the counting (e.g. due to the presence floating nucleic acid in droplets that do not meet the filgered_cell condition). While this procedure is explained in the paper, I realize it will be worth to explicitly explain this in the docs, as well. I will put it on my todo list.

The correct way to deal with this custom analysis would be to use the option ``-b/--bcfile``, this right now is only available for the command ``velocyto run`` and not ``velocyto run10X``, but I think it makes sense since this will be a customized analysis not the standard 10X. We could add the option `-b/--bcfile` to `run10x` but right now I meant run10x as more of a shortcut that assumes the use of the output/folder structure from ``cellranger`` but eventually people should use the mroe general ``run``.

Given recent updates to expose events in the local provider, I believe this can be fixed relatively easily by looking at the timestamp of the first event in `events.txt` [here](https://github.com/DataBiosphere/dsub/blob/master/dsub/providers/local.py#L922) instead of just using the create-time.

Ok, so in fact the grammar I wrote is traduced from a BNF grammar in which there are 2 types of rules : 
`rule1: "-" "FIELD" X` meaning `"-" (SEP)* "FIELD" (SEP)* X` where SEP is a whitespace or \n or \r
`rule2: day ! month` meaning `day (SEP){0} month` where day and month are a 2 digit number.

So for the first rule, "- FIELD X", "-  (10 whitespace here)   FIELD (30 here)        X" and "-FIELD   \n   X" should match,
and for the second one, "0107" matches, but not "01 07".

If I ignore whitespaces in my grammar, the second rule fails, and if I don't ignore them, I have to write every separators in rules like the first one (`"-" SEP "FIELD" SEP X`) and don't write them in rules like the second one (`day month`).

Writing every separator is slowing a lot the grammar and is absolutely not readable.
I may be mistaken but I don't see how to do this without a concatenation operator.

Do you see what I mean?

This should still update the environment to add bin (or Scripts on windows) to your PATH. This is in keeping with the principal that this activation will extend/override, but not replace a previous activation as denoted in the documentation. It will meanrly cause items to first be searched for in this environment which includes BOTH the modules, and the top level scripts.

To do otherwise causes many more problems. For instance if you use activate_this.py and then try to spawn a call to sphinx-build, it will use the executable from the old env, as that is what is on the PATH, but the module from the new env, as that is what is on the PYTHONPATH.

Hmm, on second thought this is a more involved change. `virtualenv_support` is an actual package that is installed with package-data, and it would be bad to have `site.py` and the other `.py` files in there installed with virtualenv, as that would make it possible for a user to "import virtualenv_support.site" with strange and undesired results.

What could/should be done is to split out the files that are not really part of the virtualenv_support package, but are just used for rebuilding virtualenv.py (site.py, distutils-init.py, etc) into a different directory entirely. Then they could be included in the sdist via MANIFEST.in without risking them being installed or importable. I don't consider this high priority but I'd accept a pull request for it.
